text: SMOPCA: spatially aware dimension reduction integrating multi-omics improves the efficiency of spatial domain detection. Mo Chen, Ruihua Cheng, Jianuo He, Jun Chen, Jie Zhang. Genome Biology(2025) 26:135. Abstract: Technological advances have enabled us to profile multiple omics layers with spatial information, significantly enhancing spatial domain detection and advancing a variety of biomedical research fields. Despite these advancements, there is a notable lack of effective methods for modeling spatial multi-omics data. We introduce SMOPCA, a Spatial Multi-Omics Principal Component Analysis method designed to perform joint dimension reduction on multimodal data while preserving spatial dependencies. Extensive experiments reveal that SMOPCA outperforms existing single-modal and multimodal dimension reduction and clustering methods, across both single-cell and spatial multi-omics datasets derived from diverse technologies and tissue structures.

Background: Cellular phenotype and functional states are intrinsically regulated at multiple “omics” layers. The rapid development in multimodal sequencing technologies enables us to simultaneously profile different omics layers, providing a complete representation of cellular identity. Recent advances in spatial multi-omics technologies further allow us to profile different aspects of a cell while retaining spatial information, and are expected to improve spatial domain detection. To fully exploit the complementary nature of single-cell multi-omics data, joint dimension reduction and clustering analysis algorithms have been proposed. However, to our best knowledge, none of the aforementioned methods have the capability to model spatial dependencies in the data. Therefore, current computational methods for single-cell multi-omics data may not be optimal for analyzing spatial multi-omics datasets, as they assume independence among cells or spots and fail to integrate crucial spatial information.

Recently developed methods for analyzing spatially resolved transcriptomics (SRT) data (e.g., SpatialPCA, SpaVAE) may not be directly applicable to spatial multi-omics data. Methods like SpatialGlue can only process at most two modalities. MEFISTO can natively handle data with three or more modalities, but its performance is less competitive. Methods that can effectively and simultaneously model various data modalities and spatial information are scarce. To fill the methodological gap, we introduce SMOPCA, a novel spatial multi-omics principal component analysis method. SMOPCA simultaneously models different data modalities and spatial information and infers a joint low-dimensional representation over multiple omics data types. The latent factors of SMOPCA encapsulate variations across data modalities. The learned latent representations maintain the spatial correlation structure across tissue locations. SMOPCA is readily applicable to model single-cell multi-omics data with pseudo-spatial coordinates generated from UMAP.

section: SMOPCA Model Framework and Core Mathematical Formulation
text: We consider a spatial multi-omics dataset with n spatial locations (spots or cells) having known spatial coordinates s_i. Let k∈{1,...,K} denote the modality. Y_k is the m_k×n measurement matrix of the kth modality. We aim to learn a joint d×n latent factor matrix Z. The model is: Y_k = W_k Z + E_k, (1) where W_k is an m_k×d factor loading matrix; and E_k is an m_k×n residual error matrix with E_{k_{ji}} ∼ N(0, σ_k^2). Different data modalities share the same latent factor matrix Z. To model spatial dependencies, we assume each latent factor vector Z_l follows a multivariate normal distribution: Z_l ∼ MVN(0, Σ_l), l=1,...,d, (2) where Σ_l is an n×n covariance matrix constructed using a kernel function defined over spatial coordinates. By default, we use the Matern kernel (v=3/2): k_{v=3/2,γ}(s_i, s_j) = (1 + (√3 ||s_i - s_j||)/γ) exp(-(√3 ||s_i - s_j||)/γ), (3) where γ is the length scale parameter.

Inference: SMOPCA uses a Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) framework. We assume Σ_1 = ... = Σ_d = Σ. The modality-specific parameters (W_k, σ_k^2) and the shared parameter γ are estimated iteratively. Users can specify modality-dependent weights α_k. The posterior expectation of Z, used as the final low-dimensional representation, is derived as: Ẑ_l^T = Σ_{k=1}^K ( Σ_{k=1}^K (α_k/σ̂_k^2) I_n + Σ^{-1} )^{-1} ( (α_k/σ̂_k^2) Y_k^T ŵ_{k_l} ). (6) Implementation: SMOPCA is implemented in Python 3. The latent space dimension d is set to 20 by default. The length scale parameter γ is learned automatically. K-means clustering is performed on the low-dimensional vectors. Data Preprocessing: For each modality, genes/proteins with zero counts are filtered. The count matrix is normalized by library size, followed by log transformation and scaling to zero mean and unit variance. For ATAC data, reads are mapped to gene regions to create a gene activity matrix, which is then normalized similarly. For spatial data, spatially variable genes (SVGs) are selected using SPARK (FDR ≤ 0.05).

section: Simulation Study I: Single-Cell Multi-Omics with Simulated Spatial Coordinates
text: To evaluate SMOPCA, we conducted simulation experiments using real single-cell multi-omics datasets (five CITE-seq and two SMAGE-seq datasets) with simulated cell locations. We assumed cells from the same cluster are proximate, inspired by layered brain tissue patterns. For each real dataset, we generated 10 simulated location sets. We compared SMOPCA against methods including BREM-SC, CiteFuse, scMDC, Seurat, TotalVI, SpatialPCA, and PCA+K-means. Evaluation metrics: Adjusted Rand Index (ARI), Normalized Mutual Information (NMI), and Adjusted Mutual Information (AMI). Results: SMOPCA and SpatialPCA outperformed PCA+K-means, highlighting the efficacy of using (simulated) spatial information. SMOPCA outperformed SpatialPCA (which takes concatenated data as input) and all other methods, including the leading deep learning method scMDC, across all three metrics on both CITE-seq and SMAGE-seq datasets. SMOPCA also outperformed SpaVAE and SpatialGlue on these datasets. Sensitivity analysis showed SMOPCA is robust to different kernels, parameters, number of components, and preprocessing methods.

section: Simulation Study II & Application to Spatial Multi-Omics Data Part 1
text: Simulation Study II: To demonstrate capability with >2 modalities, we simulated datasets with three modalities using SRTsim based on the LIBD human DLPFC data (12 tissue sections). SMOPCA outperformed PCA+K-means, SpatialPCA, SpaVAE, and SpatialGlue_3M, with statistically significant differences. Application to Spatial CITE-seq Data: We applied SMOPCA to a real spatial-CITE-seq dataset of human tonsil tissue. We compared with scMDC, Seurat, TotalVI, SpatialPCA, MEFISTO, SpaVAE, and SpatialGlue. Clustering patterns from SMOPCA were spatially smoother and more concordant with the tissue image. SMOPCA achieved the highest spatial smoothness (Moran's I score) and lowest mixing (LISI score). We annotated clusters (e.g., crypt epithelia, T cell zones, germinal center dark/light zones) and performed differential expression analysis, identifying upregulated marker genes and proteins consistent with known biology (e.g., CD3, CD4 in T cell zones; CD21, CD23 in GC B cells).

section: Application to Spatial Multi-Omics Data Part 2 (Stereo-CITE-seq, MISAR-seq) and Single-Cell Data
text: Application to Stereo-CITE-seq Data: Analysis of a mouse thymus section. SMOPCA, scMDC, MEFISTO, SpaVAE, and SpatialGlue detected similar biological patterns. SMOPCA achieved the best Moran's I and LISI scores. Application to Spatial ATAC and mRNA Data (MISAR-seq): Analysis of a mouse embryonic (E15.5) brain dataset. SMOPCA and SpatialPCA yielded notably smoother results than other methods. SMOPCA consistently delivered the best AMI, NMI, and ARI scores. SMOPCA also had the highest McFadden-adjusted pseudo R² value, indicating its latent representations best predict true cell types. Application to Single-Cell Multi-Omics Data: SMOPCA is applicable to single-cell multi-omics data using pseudo-spatial coordinates generated from UMAP (applied to top variable genes). On real CITE-seq and SMAGE-seq datasets with UMAP coordinates, SMOPCA consistently yielded superior or comparable results to scMDC and outperformed many other methods. A special version of SMOPCA with a diagonal covariance matrix still delivered good results, showing flexibility.

Discussion: SMOPCA explicitly integrates spatial location into learned latent factors. The lengthscale parameter γ controls spatial pattern smoothness. Unlike SpatialPCA which uses heuristics, SMOPCA learns γ automatically via MLE. We assume Σ_1=...=Σ_d=Σ for computational convenience, leading to a closed-form solution for Ŵ_k. Different kernels (Matern, Cauchy, Gaussian) can be used; SMOPCA is not very sensitive to this choice. Current time/space complexity is O(n³) and O(n²), similar to SpatialPCA. SMOPCA uses normalized data; future work could model count data directly. Conclusions: SMOPCA is a powerful, flexible dependency-aware dimension reduction framework for spatial multi-omics. It preserves neighborhood similarity in the latent space and captures spatial correlations across omics layers. Experiments demonstrate it delivers competitive or superior performance with enhanced efficiency and robustness. Data and Code Availability: All datasets are publicly available. SMOPCA is available on GitHub: https://github.com/cmhimself/SMOPCA and Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15187362.
